published_figure: Idaho 2023 production: 147,400 thousand cwt = 6,682 t/wk average
derivation: Model represents one sub-regional processing corridor. 300 t/wk = 4.5% of Idaho average weekly production. Scale chosen so modelled storage nodes operate at ~55-65% utilisation baseline.

published_figure: WA 2023: 62,250 thou cwt; ID 2023: 147,400 thou cwt → ratio=0.422
derivation: Washington / Idaho tonnage ratio. Model scale (0.65) slightly higher than pure tonnage ratio to reflect WA's higher value-per-acre share.
Oregon/Idaho ratio is 0.125; model uses 0.45 to account for Oregon's role as a processing-potato hub with higher per-unit flow than raw tonnage suggests.
Colorado/Idaho ratio 0.11; model 0.30 reflects CO as Mountain regional supplier with shorter supply chain.

published_figure: Total potato consumption: 54.1 kg/person/year. West Coast metro population: ~21.5M. Retail share: 55%.
derivation: 21.5M × 54.1 kg × 0.55 / 1000 / 52 = 12,303 t/wk actual. Model uses 230 t/wk = 1.9% — same corridor-slice fraction as production.

source_doc: Vegetables and Pulses Outlook / NASS Processing Use
published_figure: ~45% of US potatoes go to processing; western plants handle ~60% of processing volume
derivation: Set at 75% of proc_west throughput_capacity (560 t/wk) so processor operates under genuine pull-demand without being inherently saturated. Processor demand is not purely per-capita — it reflects contracted throughput commitments.

published_figure: Eastern processors handle ~40% of US processing volume
derivation: 75% of proc_east throughput_capacity (420 t/wk). Demand:capacity ratio kept constant across processors.

source_doc: FAF5 2022 OD Data; FHWA NHPN highway network
published_figure: Idaho→Los Angeles: ~1,050 highway miles. Average truck speed 55 mph, 10 hrs/day = ~1.9 days ≈ 2 weeks
derivation: 1050 miles / (55 mph × 10 hr/day) = 1.9 days. Rounded to 2-step transit (weekly model). Idaho→processor is the primary fresh-potato corridor.

published_figure: Idaho→Chicago: ~1,780 miles → 3.2 days. Idaho→NYC: ~2,350 miles → 4.3 days.
derivation: Eastern processor route longer; 4-step transit captures the 2-4 day range for Idaho→Midwest/East corridors. Slightly conservative vs FAF5 average to account for produce-route scheduling delays.
Intra-state short-haul. 1-step transit = within same week.

unit: fraction of near_expiry cohort per step
source_doc: PACA Good Delivery Guidelines; ARS Agric. Handbook 66 (2016)
published_figure: PACA: 14-day max non-refrigerated transit. ARS HB66: 2-3 week ambient shelf life for table stock.
derivation: At 0.28/step: 50% of near_expiry stock is spoiled after 2.5 steps (2.5 weeks). This aligns with the ~2-week PACA non-refrigerated transit window after which significant quality loss is expected.

unit: multiplier on all aging transition rates
source_doc: ARS Agricultural Handbook Number 66 (2016), Potato section pp. 453-461
published_figure: Storage life at 7-10°C: 5-8 months vs 2-3 weeks at ambient. Ratio: ~8-16× slower quality loss in cold storage.
derivation: Model uses 1/0.35 ≈ 2.9× slowdown — conservative relative to ideal cold storage (8×) to represent real-world commercial cold-chain with loading/unloading thermal cycling and imperfect temperature maintenance.

source_doc: ARS Agricultural Handbook 66; PACA quality standards
published_figure: Sprouting begins 2-4 weeks at room temperature. No sharp PACA threshold for fresh→mid_life.
derivation: Calibrated so fresh inventory reaches mid_life over 5-6 steps (weeks). Mid_life represents 'acceptable but not premium' quality — still within PACA good-delivery grade but showing visible signs of aging.

published_figure: Quality deterioration accelerates after 6-8 weeks ambient.
derivation: Calibrated so a unit of fresh inventory becomes near_expiry after approximately 8-10 steps (weeks) in warm storage, consistent with ARS Handbook guidance on ambient potato quality decline.
